name: B.S. in Web & Information Systems

dimensions.name: Curriculum coherence & progression
dimensions.justification: The plan of study is well sequenced term over term with a clear web/IS spine: CS 100 -> CS 113 -> CS 114/IT 114 for programming, and IS 117 (Intro Website Development) -> IS 218 (Building Web Applications) -> IS 219 (Adv Website Development) -> IS 421 (Advanced Web Applications) for the web track, plus a coherent UX progression (IS 247 -> IS 375 -> IS 448 Usability & Measuring UX). Foundations (calculus, statistics, ENGL 101/102) land early and advanced electives (IS 322, IS 333, IS 373, IS 392) in later years. The main weakness is that only 7 of the expected 8 semesters are present — the Fourth Year 2nd (final) semester is absent from the data — leaving the capstone term of the progression unverifiable.
dimensions.suggestion: Restore the missing Fourth Year 2nd (final) semester so the full eight-term progression, including any senior capstone, is visible and can be checked end to end.

dimensions.name: Prerequisite structure health
dimensions.justification: The prerequisite graph is mostly sensible and uses explicit boolean structure (e.g., IS 218: '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)') with grade thresholds, and foundational courses correctly show no prerequisites. However metrics.deepestPrereqChain is 7 (IS 333 sits at depth 7), which is on the deep side and partly inflated by long GUR-style prerequisite lists, and metrics.missingCourseCount is 4 with missingCoursesSample [GER 200, GER 300, YWCC 207, YWCC 307]: GER 200/GER 300 are General-Education-Requirement placeholders mis-read as course codes, while YWCC 207 and YWCC 307 are real required 1-credit professional-development courses that dangle because they are absent from the undergraduate catalog.
dimensions.suggestion: Add catalog entries for YWCC 207 and YWCC 307 (or link the correct codes) and treat GER 200/GER 300 as GER placeholders rather than course codes, then re-check whether the depth-7 chain reflects genuine sequencing or over-constraint from bundled GUR lists.

dimensions.name: Learning outcomes & objectives
dimensions.justification: The provided data contains no program-level learning outcomes or educational objectives — only the plan-of-study term structure and course descriptions. For a catalog-quality page, the absence of stated outcomes is a real gap: a prospective student cannot see what graduates are expected to know or be able to do.
dimensions.suggestion: Publish an explicit, numbered list of program-level learning outcomes / educational objectives (e.g., design and build web-based information systems, apply UX research and usability evaluation, manage databases) so the page states measurable expected competencies.

dimensions.name: Credit distribution & balance
dimensions.justification: Credit transparency is compromised: statedTotalCredits is null (the plan-of-study total did not parse cleanly) and metrics.creditReconcileDelta is null, so the plan cannot be reconciled to a stated total. The seven visible terms sum to only 108 credits (metrics.summedTermCredits = 108) — well short of a typical ~120-124 credit B.S. — consistent with the missing final semester. Per-term loads among the visible terms are balanced (15-16 credits each) and the major/GER/elective mix looks reasonable, but the missing total and missing term make overall balance impossible to confirm.
dimensions.suggestion: Parse and display the official stated total credits and include the final term so summedTermCredits reconciles to that total (delta = 0), giving a transparent, verifiable credit distribution.

dimensions.name: Presentation & clarity
dimensions.justification: The core course rows are clean, but the plan-of-study parsing introduces noticeable garbling: General-Education-Requirement rows are split across multiple items with catalog URLs bleeding into the course list (e.g., 'Science Literacy GER (http://catalog.njit.edu/...' followed by a stray 'education-requirements/natural-science-ger/)' row), alternative-course lines appear as orphan items ('or CS 114 or Introduction to Computer Science II'), and several titles are abbreviated ('Adv Website Development', 'Computing & Effective Com'). Combined with the null total credits and the missing final semester, the page reads as incompletely cleaned.
dimensions.suggestion: Post-process the plan of study to collapse split GER/URL fragments into single labeled placeholder rows, fold 'or ...' alternatives into their parent course, and expand truncated titles for a clean, readable table.

dimensions.name: Industry & currency relevance
dimensions.justification: The curriculum is strongly current and industry-aligned. It covers modern web engineering (Building Web Applications, Advanced Web Applications, Content Management Systems), mobile (IS 322 Mobile Applications), data-facing skills (IS 331 Database Design, IS 392 Web Mining and Information Retrieval, IS 333 Social Network Analysis), a full UX arc (Designing the User Experience, Discovering User Needs for UX, Usability & Measuring UX), plus E-Commerce Technology and professional/ethics content (IS 350, COM 312/313, ENTR/IE management). This maps directly to real web, UX, and information-systems roles.
dimensions.suggestion: Keep the strong UX and web-engineering core and consider surfacing current stack/tooling (e.g., cloud deployment, APIs, front-end frameworks) explicitly in course descriptions to signal currency to prospective students.

descriptionQuality.justification: Across the 38 sampled courses, descriptions are consistently present and substantive — the shown excerpts run the full ~220-character sample window with concrete, informative content (e.g., IS 218 'a critical, hands-on introduction to the design of Web-based Information Systems', IS 392 covering 'design, implementation and evaluation of search engines and web mining applications'). No missing or stub descriptions were observed; a minor deduction is that some entries lead with restriction/prerequisite prose before describing the course.

prerequisiteClarity.justification: Prerequisite text is generally clear and machine-parseable, with explicit boolean structure and grade thresholds (e.g., IS 218/IS 219 '(IS 117 or IT 202) and (CS 100, CS 113, or CS 115)', CS 113 'CS 100 or CS 103 with a grade C or better'), and true foundation courses correctly carry no prerequisite. Clarity dips only where GUR requirements are inlined as long alternation lists (IS 333, IS 350), which are accurate but verbose and harder to read at a glance.

topSuggestions: Publish explicit program-level learning outcomes / educational objectives — none are present in the data.
Fix credit transparency: parse and show the official stated total credits and add the missing Fourth Year 2nd (final) semester so the 108 summed credits reconcile to the real total (delta = 0).
Clean up plan-of-study presentation: collapse split GER rows and bleeding catalog URLs, fold 'or ...' alternatives into their parent course, and expand truncated course titles.

Resolve the 4 dangling references — add catalog entries for the required YWCC 207 and YWCC 307 professional-development courses, and treat GER 200 / GER 300 as GER placeholders rather than course codes.
Review the depth-7 prerequisite chain (e.g., IS 333) to confirm it reflects genuine sequencing rather than over-constraint from bundled GUR prerequisite lists.
